summary:
The Pueblo Revolt (August 10, 1680 AD) was the most successful indigenous uprising against European colonialism in North American history, in which the Pueblo peoples of modern-day New Mexico — led by the Tewa religious leader Popé — coordinated a simultaneous uprising across 24 Pueblo towns against Spanish colonial rule, killing approximately 400 Spaniards (including 21 of 33 Franciscan missionaries) and forcing the complete evacuation of the remaining 2,000 Spanish colonists from New Mexico for 12 years. The revolt responded to over 80 years of Spanish colonial oppression: forced labor, suppression of Pueblo religion (Popé himself had been arrested and publicly flogged for allegedly practicing indigenous ceremonies in 1675), forced tribute, and famine caused by Spanish cattle herds consuming Pueblo agricultural lands.

Popé organized the revolt from Taos Pueblo, coordinating through knotted cords sent secretly to participating Pueblo leaders — a remarkable feat of inter-tribal coordination across several different language groups and historically competing communities. The date of revolt was moved up when the Spanish discovered two Pueblo runners carrying information; Popé ordered the revolt launched the next day rather than risk further delay. The Spanish governor Otermin survived the initial attacks, retreated to El Paso, and attempted two reconquests (1681, 1688) both of which failed. New Mexico was not reconquered until 1692 (the 'peaceful' reconquest by Diego de Vargas). The revolt's long-term consequence was significant: the returning Spanish made substantial concessions, reducing labor demands, permitting Pueblo religious practices, and abandoning the encomienda system — demonstrating that indigenous resistance could force modifications in colonial policy.
